author: Early Corpus Analyst Cedar

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: The player becomes a scale-shifting craft hero who notices hidden communities and transforms ordinary objects into large navigational problems by becoming tiny.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: The cadence returns through a lively town hub, follows elemental dungeon goals, acquires tools and sword powers, shifts scale, and resolves optional Kinstone opportunities.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Sword actions, rolling, lifting, shrinking and growing, item tools, gliding, digging, swimming, vacuuming, and temporary duplication support dense top-down interaction.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Scale, fused Kinstone pairs, opened remote events, restored sword powers, dungeon completion, NPC services, shortcuts, and collectibles persist or trigger visible changes.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: A compact overworld and central town contain layered human-scale and Minish-scale routes; portals convert familiar props, cracks, and interiors into a second local topology.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Shrink portals, new tools, sword techniques, elemental progression, and Kinstone outcomes reopen cracks, water, soil, wind routes, simultaneous switches, and town interiors.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Scale contrast, matching Kinstone shapes, repeated tool targets, clone panels, and room geometry communicate rule hypotheses through visible compatibility.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: Elemental dungeons use room dependencies, a signature item, traversal escalation, a boss, and an element reward that upgrades the campaign's central sword objective.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Small passages, Kinstone fusions, figurines, heart pieces, shells, sword teachers, caves, and changed remote locations make social and spatial curiosity materially productive.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Heart capacity, rupees, shells, Kinstone pieces, item upgrades, bottles, sword techniques, and equipment improvements create several collectible and capability tracks.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Top-down combat combines sword timing with tool effects and movement, while bosses use scale, arena state, or recently practiced item behavior as readable constraints.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: Town residents and hidden Minish provide services, requests, clues, instruction, and Kinstone fusion partners whose matched pieces can cause events elsewhere.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: A cursed companion cap comments during travel, while festival framing, scale reveals, elemental milestones, and sword restoration connect character scenes to acquired abilities.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: Top-down rooms keep interactions readable; shrinking effects, zoomed scale cues, doorway cuts, and remote-event glimpses stage how the same world changes in size or state.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Early scale portals place an obvious tiny route beside an impossible human-scale obstacle, and each tool or fusion produces an immediate animation plus a visible consequence.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: Two assignable item buttons, a context action, roll and sword controls, inventory screens, Kinstone bag, and collectible displays organize a large verb set on Game Boy Advance.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: Persistent saves, bottles and fairies, compact returns, and re-usable scale portals support retries; puzzle states generally preserve a path back to a legal size and position.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: The 2D top-down view expresses scale by swapping collision and art relationships rather than camera depth, making puddles, books, holes, and furniture into different route classes.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: The original Game Boy Advance title is single-player; its wider color display and additional shoulder buttons support dense scenes, rolling, and a broad assignable inventory.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: Its principles require dual-scale collision layers, portal-safe transforms, remote event flags, NPC fusion tables, temporary clone simulation, tool gates, persistent collectibles, and return-path validation.

observationThemes.context: At a designated portal, Link can change between human and Minish scale while nearby ordinary scenery remains recognizable.
preconditions: A valid scale-change portal is accessible
The destination route is compatible with the target size
playerInput: Traverse a crack, tiny doorway, prop surface, or Minish route
Return to a portal and grow when needed
before: At human scale, a small opening is inaccessible and minor scenery is non-navigable
The player sees the broad route but not its tiny path
action: Change scale and move through the reclassified environment
after: Tiny passages and characters become accessible
Ordinary hazards such as water or height become proportionally significant
feedback: A clear transformation animation and changed sprite scale mark the state
Collision and path width immediately confirm which features now matter
consequences: The player reaches information, tools, progression, or collectibles
Scale change becomes a recurring exploration verb rather than a one-time spectacle
observationThemes.factualDescription: Using designated portals changes Link between human and Minish size, causing cracks, tiny entrances, props, puddles, and small characters to have different traversal or interaction roles.
observationThemes.interpretation: Scale fantasy becomes systemic when familiar scenery retains identity but changes affordance class, letting the player repeatedly rediscover one place through a different body-world relationship.

observationThemes.context: An NPC displays a compatible Kinstone half, and the player holds a matching piece collected elsewhere in the world.
preconditions: The character is available for fusion
A matching Kinstone piece is present in the player's bag
playerInput: Select and join the matching piece
Travel to the location shown by the resulting event
before: A remote chest, passage, creature, or opportunity has not appeared
after: A remote world location changes state
feedback: A cutaway or map-like glimpse shows the distant change and its location
consequences: The player gains a concrete revisit target
Social curiosity is converted into spatial exploration and reward
observationThemes.factualDescription: Matching a carried Kinstone piece with a compatible NPC records the fusion and triggers one of many remote world changes, which is shown before the player travels to inspect it.
observationThemes.interpretation: Showing the distant consequence makes a social collectible causal rather than abstract, turning an NPC encounter into a memorable invitation to revisit world topology.

observationThemes.context: An elemental dungeon introduces a signature tool through clear targets, then combines the same reaction with traversal, hazards, enemies, or its guardian arena.
preconditions: The dungeon item has been acquired
Its basic target reaction has been demonstrated
playerInput: Use the item on repeated compatible targets
Apply the rule in more demanding room states
Use learned behavior to complete the guardian encounter
before: Later rooms and the element objective are inaccessible
The player knows only the tool's initial use
action: Resolve the escalating dependency chain and boss
after: The signature item remains part of overworld traversal and combat
feedback: Target animation, movement, sound, enemy reaction, and door changes confirm each application
Boss-state changes expose whether the rule was applied correctly
consequences: New field obstacles can be approached with a practiced tool
observationThemes.factualDescription: Elemental dungeons grant persistent signature items, repeat their compatible target grammar with escalating demands, and close with a boss and element reward.
observationThemes.interpretation: A tool feels integrated when the bounded challenge develops it from readable experiment to pressured application and then returns it to the wider exploration vocabulary.

observationThemes.context: After the central blade gains sufficient restored power, marked floor panels allow Link to charge and create temporary copies in a chosen formation.
preconditions: The blade has the required restoration level
A clone panel and simultaneous targets are present
playerInput: Choose positions and charge the sword
Move the resulting formation onto simultaneous switches or through the staged route
before: One body cannot activate all required targets at once
The restored sword power has not been embodied in the room
action: Generate and control the temporary formation
after: A door, barrier, or route state changes
feedback: Copies, formation movement, switch depression, and door animation show the causal chain
consequences: The player advances through a story-linked capability gate
Sword restoration is expressed as a spatial verb as well as a narrative milestone
observationThemes.factualDescription: Restored sword power lets Link create temporary copies on marked panels; moving the formation onto multiple targets can activate simultaneous room mechanisms.
observationThemes.interpretation: A narrative artifact upgrade becomes more persuasive when its growth is represented by a new embodied coordination problem with previewable formation and visible consequence.

observationThemes.context: Scale changes occur at designated anchors in a single-player top-down map, and traversal routes must eventually provide a legal anchor for returning to required size.
preconditions: Link is in a valid size state at a portal
The destination side contains a traversable route and eventual recovery anchor
playerInput: Explore within the size-specific collision layer
Use another valid portal to restore the needed scale
before: Only one collision and interaction layer is active
Some exits or controls are incompatible with that size
action: Traverse the anchored scale-state graph and return
after: The alternate collision layer is exited safely
Persistent campaign and inventory state remain unchanged
feedback: Transformation, sprite scale, camera framing, and collision immediately show the active layer
The destination portal provides an explicit recovery point
consequences: Players can experiment with scale without becoming permanently stranded
Saved single-player progress remains valid across compact handheld sessions
observationThemes.factualDescription: The Game Boy Advance adventure restricts shrinking and growing to designated portals, linking human- and Minish-scale top-down routes while preserving persistent inventory and save state.
observationThemes.interpretation: Anchored transformations constrain a powerful world rewrite into a testable graph, making anti-softlock guarantees and return-path readability part of the mechanic's design.

contributionHypotheses: Scale change can recontextualize a familiar map when stable objects switch traversal class and each transformation has an explicit safe anchor.
A social collectible becomes a spatial hook when the game previews a persistent remote consequence and trusts the player to travel there.
Artifact restoration gains dramatic credibility when each milestone adds a distinct player-controlled spatial capability.
uniqueOrNonportable: The named tiny people, cap companion, sword, villain, festival, Kinstones, exact fusion effects, map and prop layouts, element sequence, characters, and audiovisual identity are source-specific.
The precise clone panels and scale-dependent routes are evidence only; reuse requires original transformation fiction, topology, targets, and visual language.
openQuestions: A later review should identify which optional fusion outcomes are guaranteed versus inventory-dependent so coverage claims do not overstate deterministic progression.
